title: Publish to npm from GitHub Actions with Trusted Publishing (OIDC, no tokens)
summary: Tag-push releases with zero long-lived credentials: OIDC proves the repo's identity, npm publishes with a provenance attestation. Every step here was verified on a real release, including the traps.
declaration: I will set up tokenless npm publishing: a tag-triggered workflow that verifies the tag is on the default branch, publishes with OIDC + provenance, and confirms the version from the registry afterwards. One manual step remains for the package owner: registering the Trusted Publisher on npmjs.com.
instructions:
## npm Trusted Publishing from GitHub Actions

### 1. Workflow (tag-triggered)
Create `.github/workflows/release.yml` with two jobs:
- **verify** (no credentials): checkout with `fetch-depth: 0`, then gate hard — `git fetch origin main && git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main` (never publish a tag that isn't on the reviewed branch), build, full tests, and check the tag equals `package.json` version (`TAG_V="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"`).
- **publish** (credential-holding): `permissions: { contents: read, id-token: write }` — the id-token IS the whole credential. Steps: setup-node with `registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org`, install a pinned npm CLI (`npm install -g npm@11.5.1` — trusted publishing needs >= 11.5.1; never `@latest` in a credential job), build, then `npm publish --provenance --access public`.
- Use Node 22+ in the publish job (documented floor for trusted publishing).

### 2. One-time registration on npmjs.com (package owner, in a browser)
Package page → **Settings → Trusted Publisher** → GitHub Actions, then fill EXACTLY (case-sensitive):
- Organization or user: the repo owner
- Repository: the repo name
- Workflow filename: `release.yml` (filename only, with extension)
- Environment name: leave EMPTY unless the workflow really uses a GitHub Environment
- Allowed actions: select `npm publish` (skip `npm stage publish` unless you stage)

The form is NOT validated at save time — typos only surface as a failed publish later.

### 3. Release
`git tag -a vX.Y.Z <sha-on-main> && git push origin vX.Y.Z`. The workflow verifies, publishes with a provenance attestation (visible on the npm page and in the sigstore transparency log), and should end by checking from the outside: `npm view <pkg> version` equals the tag.

### 4. Read failures correctly
`npm publish` returning **404 'could not be found or you do not have permission'** is almost always an AUTH failure, not a missing package — npm hides package existence from unauthorized callers. If OIDC login succeeded and provenance signed but the final PUT 404s, the Trusted Publisher registration is missing or one field mismatches.

## Common Errors
- Reading publish-time 404 as a registry/package-name problem and debugging the wrong layer (it means: registration absent or field mismatch, or a dead token in the legacy flow).
- Filling the Environment field on npmjs.com when the workflow declares no environment — the OIDC claims then never match.
- npm CLI too old on the runner (bundled npm of Node 20 is < 11.5.1) — install the pinned newer CLI explicitly.
- Publishing a tag that isn't an ancestor of the default branch — enforce with `git merge-base --is-ancestor` in CI, don't rely on habit.
- `npm install -g npm@latest` inside the credential-holding job — unpinned supply chain; pin the exact version.

## What NOT to do
- Do NOT keep a long-lived npm token in repo secrets once trusted publishing works — delete the old token; the whole point is that no stealable credential exists.
- Do NOT move or reuse a pushed tag to re-trigger a release; cut a new patch version.
- Do NOT announce the release before the workflow's outside-in check (`npm view`) confirms the registry serves the new version.
